**Writing Guide • January 2026** Brand, Marketing & Communications Writing Guide Version 1.1 • January 2026 **1** **Before we get started…** **Writing Guide • January 2026** ## Before we get started… ABN AMRO is there for everyone who wants to move forward. We encourage action and are willing to address difficult topics. Our communications are personal, warm and attentive, so that clients feel understood. We also make our expertise accessible, to help people move ahead with practical solutions. This is the mindset behind our writing. But how do you put that into practice? ## **How to use this Writing Guide** Whether you’re working on an email, letter or web page, this guide can be a useful reference document. If you can’t remember exactly what you’re supposed to do with compound words, abbreviations or gender-neutral pronouns, the contents page and index will take you straight to the information you need. If you’re engaging an external copywriter, always send them this Writing Guide. This document will make your work easier, your texts better and your message clearer. If you have any questions about the Writing Guide or suggestions, email them to redactieraad@nl.abnamro.com Even the most experienced writers find it tricky at times, because there are so many choices you can make in the English language. It’s important to ABN AMRO that our communications are clear, transparent and well-considered. As a writer, you might find yourself walking a tightrope. If you’re not careful, your language may become reserved, woolly and superficial, while at the other end of the scale it could be too blunt or pushy. The perfect balance is right in the middle: forward-thinking, engaged and smart. This Writing Guide sets out all the rules, guidance and tips you need to get the conversation started. This ensures we always write inclusively, clearly, consistently and correctly. ## **Publication details** Brand, Marketing & Communications Thomas Elfrink **Commissioned by Design** Thomas Elfrink This Writing Guide is an ABN AMRO publication **2** **Writing Guide • January 2026May 2024** Click on a heading to go straight to the page 13 13 14 14 15 15 15 **2** 16 **20** 21 ## Contents 1/2 ## **Before we get started…** ## **1. 46| |---|---| **4** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **5** ## 1. Prepare your text Who are you writing for and why? At ABN AMRO, we help anyone with a drive to move forward by providing accessible expertise with a human touch. Written language is a key tool in this. We write lots of different texts for a range of target groups: from letters to retirees to social media posts for young entrepreneurs. Your objective and target audience should be the starting point of every text you write. **==> picture [239 x 59] intentionally omitted <==** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Prepare your text** ## **1.1 Define your target audience: who are you writing for?** When you write a text, you need to know who you are writing for. The more you know about your readers, the easier it is to align your message with their knowledge, background and expectations. Try to answer at least the following questions before you start writing: - **What does the reader know about the subject?** - An experienced investor doesn’t need to be told the difference between equities and bonds. ## **Objective:** **I want to:** inform instruct convince the reader will know [...] the reader will be able to [...] the reader will think that [...] the reader will take action [...] the reader will feel [...] set something in motion create a feeling Texts often have more than one objective, and that’s fine. But be careful not to cram too many messages into your text or it may become confusing. It should always be clear to your readers what they need to do after reading your text. - **How does the reader feel about the subject?** If you are communicating a negative or sensitive message, explain it carefully and show that you understand the reader and their point of view. - **What are the characteristics of your target audience?** What is their average age, for example? You should use a different tone to address young people than an older audience. Young people are also more likely to be familiar with the online possibilities we offer. ## **Example: think of questions about your text** ABN AMRO wants to receive annual results in digital format only, and will no longer accept hard-copy versions. You are writing a letter to business clients on this. Your aim is to get them to accept the change. **Clients could ask a range of questions:** ## **1.2 Define your objective: why are you writing this text?** The information you provide must always enable your readers to take the next step. But what exactly do they need to do when they have read your text? Formulate your objectives in terms of the behaviour you want to elicit. 1. Why does ABN AMRO insist on receiving my annual results in digital format? 2. What are the advantages (and disadvantages) for me? 3. What if I can only submit a hard-copy version of my annual results? 4. Can my accountant submit my annual results in digital format? 5. Where can I find more information about this topic? **6** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Prepare your text** ## **1.3 Determine the content: always keep your readers in mind** Once you’ve defined the target audience and objective of your text, it’s time to develop your story. Only provide the information readers need in order to take the required action. This can include things like arguments, information that answers potential questions, or a practical explanation. Strike the right balance between what you _can tell_ readers and what your readers _want to know_ . For instance, try to think of questions your readers may have about your message. A good text should usually answer at least the following questions: who, what, where, when, why and how? ## **1.4 Determine your means of communication: what form will your text take?** There are specific rules and guidelines for some types of text. For instance, a letter will look different from a text on our website. ## **1.5 Templates** Templates for presentations, newsletters and reports are available in PowerPoint and Word. Templates for letters are available in Word. You can also find our templates in the Brand Bank on SharePoint. **7** **Writing Guide • January 2026** ## 2. Structure How to structure a text carefully A good structure is the basis for a clear, effective text. This chapter gives tips on how to develop a story, from the title to the last full stop. **==> picture [239 x 59] intentionally omitted <==** **8** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Structure** ## **2.1 Think of a suitable heading** The title is a major factor in determining whether or not readers start reading your text. A good heading: - indicates what the text is about ## **2.2 Put the key message at the top** Most people don’t read texts from start to finish. They tend to quickly read the first paragraph and scan through the rest at random. So to give them the essentials right away, you need to put the most important information at the beginning of your text. - entices the reader to read on - is clear, concrete and concise The more whimsical a title is, the less clear it is. So avoid being playful. Creative titles are only acceptable if they are followed by an informative subtitle. Also bear in mind that the more prominent you make the key concepts in your text, the easier it will be to find your text. ## **2.3 Use paragraphs** Nobody wants to read large blocks of text. Paragraphs keep your text clear and easy to read. Bear these points in mind: - Don’t cram too much into your paragraphs: address one topic per paragraph Put the most important information at the top of the paragraph Put a subheading above each paragraph - - - - Use a blank line to separate paragraphs - - To make sure all paragraphs can be read individually, don’t use ‘this’ and ‘that’ to refer to things in previous paragraphs - Create paragraphs of at least three lines and no more than seven - One swallow doesn’t make a summer Why one good quarter does not make a difference to your pension investment ## **Or this** One swallow doesn’t make a summer _Why one good quarter does not make a difference to your pension investment_ **==> picture [46 x 44] intentionally omitted <==** ## **Internal communications** If you’re writing a text for ABN AMRO colleagues, you don’t need to apply the rules above and below as strictly. You don’t need to use a subheading above each paragraph, for instance. In fact, it’s better not to. And you don’t need to put the information in your paragraph in order of importance, as long as the key point is in the first sentence. **9** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Structure** ## **2.4 Use subheadings** Give every paragraph a subheading that indicates what the paragraph is about. Here are some tips for subheadings: - The reader should be able to follow the thread of your story just by reading the subheadings. - - Use bold formatting for subheadings to make them stand out. - ## **2.6 Finish with a call to action** At ABN AMRO, we want to help people move forward. If your readers are supposed to do something after they’ve read the text, finish with a clear, compelling call to action. **==> picture [46 x 43] intentionally omitted <==** ## **Check your text** - Address the reader in the heading. For example: **How will this affect you?** (not: **How will this affect me?** ) - If the heading contains a question, introduce the answer in the subsequent paragraph. - ## **How do you apply for a mortgage?** To apply for a mortgage, you first need to contact a mortgage adviser. You can then [...] ## **2.5 Use bullet points** Use bullet points to list items. This is much clearer for readers than a long string of items in the body text. Here are a few tips: - Leave a blank line between the body text and the list - - Use at least three bullet points and no more than seven - - Readers remember an uneven number of bullet points better - - If you say how many things you are going to list, use a numbered list rather than bullets - - Also use a numbered list when setting out the steps in a process - Use the Brand Experience guide via SharePoint to review your text. **==> picture [46 x 43] intentionally omitted <==** ## **Cut your word count** Your text can always be shorter. Check for unnecessary words or lengthy wording. Ask a colleague to read the text too. **==> picture [46 x 43] intentionally omitted <==** ## **Use professional translators** Use professional human translators if you are going to share your text in a language that is not your native language. You can contact the bank’s preferred suppliers for this. Machine translation tools like Word translate are good enough for non-critical communications between colleagues, but not for communications to clients, bank-wide internal communications, or branding and marketing materials. **10** **Writing Guide • January 2026** ## 3. Wording your message Style, sentences and words It’s not just _what_ you say but also _how_ you say it that determines how effective your text is. This chapter offers guidance on style, sentence construction and wording. **==> picture [239 x 59] intentionally omitted <==** **11** **Wording your message** **Writing Guide • January 2026** ## **3.1 Style** The way we write strongly affects the way people think about ABN AMRO, so it’s an important tool for communicating our brand values. Language can inspire or irritate, weaken a message or highlight painful issues. This chapter outlines the basic principles of ABN AMRO’s writing style. While these principles are applicable to all English texts for all audiences, both within and outside ABN AMRO, you should bear in mind that language usage is strongly related to your cultural environment. This is certainly true for our international network, where a text can only be effective if it takes into account the reader’s knowledge, cultural background and expectations. **==> picture [46 x 43] intentionally omitted <==** ## **Use plain language** How do you turn professional jargon into ‘ordinary’ words? That has already been done for a lot of banking and financial terms. Check out the Plain English A to Z of fnancial terms and other . Campaign’s free guides ‘ABN AMRO has a new app.’ ‘The new app makes it even easier for you to do your banking online.’ ## **Tell the story honestly** ABN AMRO communicates transparently and clearly. Never withhold information that could be relevant or interesting to your client. Be honest: point out both the advantages and disadvantages. ## **Avoid greenwashing and making unfounded claims** ## **Communicate in a personal and friendly way** We always treat our clients as equals, also in our communications. We use our texts to share information and advice, but we don’t judge the way our clients arrange their finances. We take a personal approach and are always friendly and polite. ## **Put the reader centre stage** All information must be correct and clear, and must not be misleading. Specific rules apply to sustainability claims to prevent greenwashing. Sustainability claims are all expressions relating to the sustainability of products, services or business operations in terms of their environmental, social or governance (ESG) aspects. These claims must be accurate, representative, specific, substantiated and up to date. In addition, the information must be understandable, appropriate and easy to find. To align with this, we follow the AFM’s Guidelines on Sustainability Claims. See Ruby (Confluence) for more information and examples (in Dutch). Don’t write about internal processes or products, as that will not interest your readers. Put yourself in your readers’ shoes and anticipate their questions. Why is this message useful for _them_ ? **12** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Wording your message** Question-and-answer constructions are often used in Dutch but are less common in English and should be avoided, as they can sound unnatural. ‘If you suddenly need money, you can overdraw your Privélimiet Plus account.’ ‘Your washing machine always seems to break at the worst time – when you’ve just been on an expensive holiday, for example. Our Privélimiet Plus account lets you overdraw, so you can replace your washing machine right away.’ ## **Include a call to action** Make it clear to clients what they need to do after reading your text. Always use polite language in English when you are giving instructions. ‘Do you need help? Then please contact one of our employees.’ ‘If you need help, please contact one of our employees.’ End texts with a concrete call to action: ‘Schedule a meeting’; ‘Complete the survey’; ‘Read more’. ## **3.2 Sentence construction** In a good text, every sentence hits the nail on the head. These tips will help you formulate sentences with impact. **Not like this** ‘Return this form.’ ‘Please return this form.’ ## **Write short sentences** ~~Many~~ sentences could ~~generally speaking~~ be ~~quite a bit~~ shorter. Short sentences don’t overflow with (often superfluous) information and are more readable. The risk of making mistakes in short sentences is also much smaller. As a rule of thumb, aim for sentences of 10-20 words, but make sure you vary the length. A healthy balance of long and short sentences gives a text rhythm. **13** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Wording your message** ## **How to write short sentences** - - - - Look for where you can finish a sentence and start a new one. - Ask yourself if any comma could be a full stop. - Avoid using a lot of subclauses between commas, brackets and dashes. Instead, turn a subclause into a new sentence. ‘The order has been placed by the office manager.’ ‘The survey was sent out by the customer service team.’ ‘The office manager has placed the order.’ ‘The customer service team sent out the survey.’ ‘Incurring damage might mean you need to pay demolition (or removal) charges – provided this damage is insured.’ ‘If your house has been damaged, you might need to pay demolition or removal charges. These will be refunded if your insurance covers the damage.’ ## **Don’t turn verbs into nouns** Avoid turning verbs into nouns. This style of writing makes text vague and impersonal, and you should avoid it. ## **Write active sentences** Passive sentences contain constructions like ‘has been’ + a past participle. Compare: passive, ‘You will be called’ and active, ‘We will call you’. Passive constructions make the text distant and impersonal, and it is not clear who is taking action. Active sentences are more powerful. So always say who is doing what, and when. ‘Completion of this form must be done on the same day.’ - ‘The processing of your data will take place tomorrow.’ ‘Please complete this form on the same day.’ ‘We will process your data tomorrow.’ **14** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Wording your message** ## **Avoid relative clauses** Avoid using relative clauses to give additional information about the subject of a sentence. Relative clauses make sentences unclear and difficult to read. Starting a new sentence is much more effective. ‘We have not been informed that the employer did not act according to the regulations.’ ‘As far as we are aware, the employer complied with the regulations.’ ‘The interest, which may vary monthly, is payable on the last day of the month.’ ‘The interest may vary monthly. Interest is payable on the last day of the month.’ ## **Avoid using double negatives** Double negatives should be avoided because they make sentences abstract and confusing. The reader needs to decode the sentence to understand what you are trying to say. ## **Make smart use of bullet points** You can replace complicated and lengthy explanations with a bulleted list. Lists add structure to your text and create space. Follow the guidelines in chapter 4, ‘Bullet points’. You can increase your borrowing limit if the bank considers your assets to be sufficient. You also need to fulfil the conditions of the bank’s loan policy and the additional amount you borrow should be at least €5,000. **15** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Wording your message** ## **You can increase your borrowing limit if:** - the bank considers your assets to be sufficient - - you fulfil the conditions of the bank’s loan policy - - the additional amount you borrow is at least €5,000 - If you’re writing for colleagues, other people in your line of work, or business relations who will definitely understand professional jargon, using jargon is not a problem. Giving too much explanation to these readers may come across as patronising. ## **Check your text for ambiguity** Sometimes words can be interpreted in more than one way. This can be confusing. References to things and people can also have a double meaning. Check your text for such ambiguity. ## **3.3 Choosing the right words** ## **Avoid formal language, use everyday words** Plain language uses everyday words that ‘everyone’ knows. Abstract, formal and difficult words aren’t good for your text. They make it vague, distant, unfriendly and even annoying. What’s more, formal texts often come across as old-fashioned. So use commonly used words wherever possible. If you can’t avoid using a difficult, abstract word, explain it and give an example. **‘The CEO could not call the secretary because she was not present at the time.’** Who was not present? The CEO or her secretary? Remember that business language is not the same as formal language, and everyday language is not the same as slang. You can write a business-like text without it sounding too formal. ## **Be careful about using jargon** We have a tendency to use terminology that is familiar to us, but may be hard for others to understand. Always ask yourself whether there is an easier word to use than professional jargon. And if you do have to use a difficult word, explain what it means. **16** **Wording your message** **Writing Guide • January 2026** ## **Avoid modal verbs** Try to avoid using modal verbs that express: - a probability (ABN AMRO may/can/could) - an obligation (ABN AMRO must) - a wish (ABN AMRO would like to) Such nuances are often unnecessary. A sentence is stronger if you avoid modal verbs. ‘Our employees can explain the most appropriate mortgage in your situation.’ ‘ABN AMRO is required to report to the Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets (AFM).’ ## **Tell it like it is** Words that obscure reality include verbs like ‘can’, ‘would like to’ and ‘shall’, and also expressions like ‘some’, ‘mostly’, ‘try’ and ‘likely’. We often use these because we want to be courteous or in order to weaken a message. These words, however, are vague, so they don’t make a strong impression and they make your text less clear. Only use them when absolutely necessary, from a legal perspective, for instance. The same goes for expressions like ‘etc.’, ‘a number of’ and ‘among other things’. Be specific about actions and amounts if you can. ## **Avoid figurative language** Sayings and proverbs are colourful, but they’re not precise. Some people can’t read very well or may not be native speakers of English. They probably won’t know the meaning of certain expressions, so the message will be lost on them. That is why it’s better to avoid figurative language. The section on ‘Inclusive writing’ in chapter 6 explains how to make sure your text includes everyone. ‘Our employees will be happy to explain the most appropriate mortgage in your situation.’ ‘ABN AMRO reports to the Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets (AFM).’ **17** **Wording your message** **Writing Guide • January 2026** ## **Aim for consistency** Many writers vary their choice of words. While synonyms make a text more interesting to read, too much variation will confuse your readers. That’s why it’s important to use the same word for the same term. You can use prepositions such as ‘this’, ‘that’, ‘these’ and ‘those’ instead of a synonym to make a text more varied. And if you do choose a synonym, make sure the meaning is clear. ## **Personal prepositions** - **He/she** – The idea that a person is a man or a woman is deeply engrained in our language, as our personal pronouns show. You should avoid these words. See chapter 6, ‘Inclusive writing’ for more information. - - **I** – Use ‘I’ if you are doing something for the client yourself. For example: ‘I will send you [...].’ If it is the bank doing something for the client, use ‘we’ or ‘ABN AMRO’. Preferably ‘we’, as that stands out less in the text. For example: ‘We have many years of experience with [...]’ - An exception to this principle is legal texts. In legal language, we prefer ‘the bank’ to ‘we’. - - **One** – Do not use the personal pronoun ‘one’. It is not clear who you mean, and it sounds too formal and distant. - ‘You will find the tax return form at the end of this email. You must complete the tax return form in full and then send the tax return form to your adviser.’ - ‘At the end of this email, you will find the tax return form. Please complete it in full and send it to your adviser.’ **18** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Wording your message** ## **Instructions: must, may and may not** Many of our texts instruct readers to do something. Examples include product terms and conditions for clients, or work instructions for colleagues. These texts clearly set out what is possible and permitted, and what isn’t. Use the following wording: **Reason Wording Example** Something is mandatory In policies or instructions ‘In case of an emergency to employees: ‘you must’ you must use the stairs.’ or the imperative. ‘To take advantage of this When writing to clients: offer, please make sure ‘Please make sure you…’ you return the signed or ‘Please note that you form to us.’ must…’ Something is not ‘you do not need to’ or ‘You do not need to mandatory ‘optional’ attend the meeting.’ Something is permitted ‘you may’ ‘You may use the following abbreviations in client correspondence.’ Something is prohibited ‘not permitted’ ‘Staff are not permitted to smoke on these premises.’ Something is permitted ‘only if’ ‘Use email only if […].’ under certain conditions **19** **Writing Guide • January 2026** 4 Accuracy Standards for spelling and vocabulary At ABN AMRO, we always strive to write correct and consistent texts. We want readers to take us seriously and we don’t want mistakes to distract them from content. In this chapter, we present three topics that will help you write your text according to ABN AMRO’s standards. **==> picture [239 x 59] intentionally omitted <==** **20** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Accuracy** ## **4.1 Standards for spelling and vocabulary** Spelling and vocabulary standards are listed below in alphabetical order. Our standard is to use British rather than American English. ## **Abbreviations** Abbreviations are not the best way to save space because they make a text harder to understand. Avoid abbreviating unnecessarily. If a long term is frequently used in your text, it may make your text awkward to read. In that case, abbreviating the term may be the best solution. To abbreviate a term, write out the words in full when the term is first mentioned, followed by the abbreviation in brackets; for example: ‘independent software vendor (ISV)’. After that, use only the abbreviation, but try not to repeat it too often. If an abbreviation or acronym is so familiar that it is used more often than the full form (for example: ‘US’, ‘EU’, ‘IMF’), you do not need to give the full form at all. If an organisation has formalised an abbreviation of its name, use that spelling (for example: iDEAL). **==> picture [46 x 43] intentionally omitted <==** ## **Abbreviations you may use** ## **Here are some examples:** - ‘kg’, ‘km’ (use lower case) - ‘Ltd.’ And other commonly used corporate abbreviations - Mr, Mrs, Ms (without full stop) - Currencies such as ‘USD’ and ‘EUR’; always in capital letters, although - ABN AMRO uses ‘dollars’ and ‘euros’ lower case (see under ‘Amounts’ in chapter 4.1) - **Only in informal writing:** e.g. (short for ‘exempli gratia’, which means ‘for example’); use lower case - **Only in informal writing:** i.e. (short for ‘id est’, which means ‘that is’); use lower case - **Only in internal communications:** ‘BO number’ - **Only in charts and tables:** quarterly, half-year and nine-month abbreviations of dates, for example ‘Q2’ (not: 2Q). In other texts spell out: ‘second quarter’ - ‘&’ – only use this symbol if it is part of an official name (example: EU Affairs & Market Infrastructure). It is preferable to write out the word ‘and’ instead Also see ‘Abbreviations’ in chapter 4.2, ‘Tricky words and common mistakes’. ## **ABN AMRO brand name** ‘ABN AMRO’ is used as a stand-alone brand name or together with a sub-brand or label. The following rules apply: Use the correct brand name. The complete legal name is ‘ABN AMRO Bank N.V.’ We generally use the commercial name ‘ABN AMRO’. Alternate this with ‘we’ or ‘us’ to make your texts sound less formal. Never use ‘ABN AMRO bank’. - - Do not use ‘the bank’ in texts for clients: this could refer to any bank. Legal text is the exception to the rule, when referring to the terms and conditions of our products. ‘The bank’ can be used in these cases as its meaning has been defined at the beginning of these documents. - **21** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Accuracy** - Units of the bank that are an independent legal entity are written with ‘ABN AMRO’ before the name of the unit or activity, and the first letter of the unit or activity itself is capitalised. For example: - ABN AMRO Foundation - - ABN AMRO Clearing - - ABN AMRO Hypotheken Groep - Note that names of divisions are not translated. - ‘ABN AMRO’ is not used in combination with department names. - - Do not combine our brand name ‘ABN AMRO’ with a product name. For example, write ‘ABN AMRO’s student account’ rather than ‘ABN AMRO Student Account’. - Our name must be capitalised and written in full, without a forward slash, - - underscore or hyphen. Do not write ‘ABN’, ‘ABN/AMRO’, ABN_AMRO’ or ‘ABNAMRO’. When merging component parts, do not use a hyphen in English: ‘ABN AMRO office’. - With sponsorship projects, it is essential to create a direct link between the project and our brand name. If ABN AMRO is the principal sponsor of a project, we put the name ‘ABN AMRO’ before the name of the project or event, and we capitalise the name of the sponsorship project. For example, ‘ABN AMRO World Tennis Tournament’ and ‘ABN AMRO Art Prize’. - - Never split ABN AMRO, or its sub-brands, labels or endorsements, across two lines. This also applies to ‘ABN AMRO MeesPierson’, for example. To make a non-breaking space between ‘ABN’ and ‘AMRO’: **==> picture [46 x 43] intentionally omitted <==** ## **More about the use of our brand name** Would you like to know more about how to use ‘ABN AMRO’, for example in Brand Bank on combination with our logo? Note, however, that it is ‘The Hague’. - Only use an apostrophe to indicate the possessive, as in: ‘Much has been written about the Netherlands’ unemployment rate.’ (See in this chapter ‘Apostrophes’ and ‘Possessives’.) - When putting the Netherlands in an alphabetical list, put it under the ‘N’, not the ‘T’. ## **Possessives** - As a general rule, the possessive may be used in the sense of something that may be possessed: - by a person (John Smith’s, the king’s, the prime minister’s) - - by something that can be personified (ABN AMRO’s, the company’s) - - by a proper noun (London City Council’s) - Add ’s at the end of a singular word to make it a possessive. If the word is in a plural form and already ends in ‘s’, form the possessive in the same way, but omit the second ‘s’. For example: - ‘The king’s men’ (only one king) becomes ‘the kings’ men’ (the men of several kings). - ‘The company’s assets’ becomes ‘the companies’ assets’ (the assets of several companies). Sometimes, there is one owner (singular) that ends in an ‘s’. Use an extra ‘s’ when the sound requires it. For example: - An extra ‘s’: ‘Mr Jones’s PIN’, ‘James’s account’ - No extra ‘s’: ‘The Netherlands’ flag’, ‘Socrates’ philosophy’ - ## **Practice or practise** Use the following British English spellings: ‘Practice’ is a noun, for example: - ‘Susan runs a doctor’s practice in England.’ - - ‘The bank pursues a best-practice policy in key areas.’ - ‘Practise’ is a verb, for example: - ‘I practise the piano every afternoon.’ - ## **Principal or principle** ‘Principal’ as an adjective means ‘important’ or ‘chief’, as in ‘the principal factor’. ‘The principal of a loan’ as a noun is the amount borrowed excluding interest. (Debt repayments are made by paying back the principal.) - ‘Principle’ means ‘fundamental’ or ‘general truth’ (as in: ‘in principle’). ## **Program or programme** Programme is the British English spelling and program the American spelling. Exception: always use ‘program’ when referring to computer software. **36** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Accuracy** ## **Send or sent** If it’s in the present, use ‘send’. Examples: - ‘I can send it to you right now.’ - ‘I always send my parcels from the local post office.’ Remember: it’s never ‘to sent’ – it’s always ‘to send’. Example: ## **Suggest** ‘Suggest’ is not followed by an infinitive (‘to ___’). ‘Suggest’ can be followed by a verb ending in ‘ing’, a ‘that’ clause or simply a noun. Examples: - ‘I suggest proceeding carefully in this matter.’ - - ‘I suggest that we proceed carefully in this matter.’ - - ‘I suggest caution.’ - - ‘I have asked them to send it to me as soon as possible.’ If it’s in any form of the past, use sent. Examples: - ‘I sent it to you yesterday.’ - - ‘The instructions have been sent to the bank.’ - - ‘She should have sent you the money last week.’ - ## **Than or then** Than is used when making a comparison, as in: ‘shorter than you’. Then means at that time, or over that period of time, as in: ‘First we had dinner, then we went to the cinema.’ When referring to the future both ‘send’ and ‘sent’ can show up in a phrase: - ‘I will send it to you soon.’ - ‘Your package will be sent tomorrow.’ - ‘I will have sent it to you by next Thursday.’ ## **Shall or will** This is an old distinction, more common in British English than in American English. ‘Shall’ is used primarily in legal documents; otherwise, use ‘will’. ## **Stationary or stationery** ‘Stationary’ is when something is standing still. ‘Stationery’ is a piece of paper you write a letter on. Tip: let the ‘e’ in ‘stationery’ remind you of ‘envelope’. ## **Their or there or they’re** ‘Their’ is a possessive pronoun. It is used when you want to show that something belongs to someone. For example: - ‘Their new home is in San Diego.’ - - ‘What is their phone number?’ - - ‘There’ is used to mean that something is at or in a particular place. For example: - ‘There is a present on the table.’ - - ‘Look over there to see the ocean.’ - - ‘They’re’ is a contraction for ‘they are’. For example: - ‘In my opinion, they’re a fine group of athletes.’ - - ‘They’re happy to see me.’ - **37** **Writing Guide • January 2026** **Accuracy** Deciding which word to use is easy if you remember a few simple tips: - If you can replace the questionable word with ‘his’ or ‘her’, ‘their’ is correct. - If you can replace the questionable word with ‘here’, ‘there’ is correct. - If you can replace the questionable word with ‘they are’, ‘they’re’ is correct. ## **This or that – these or those** ‘This’ is used to refer to a single person, place or thing that is close to the speaker. ‘These’ is the plural form of ‘this’. ‘That’ is used when the person, place or thing is more distant. ‘Those’ is the plural form of ‘that’. Examples: - ‘This computer is easy to use.’ - - ‘That woman over there is my boss.’ - - ‘These pictures are clearer than those.’ - ## **Whose** ## • ‘James liked the book, which had pictures.’ In the first sentence, the information after ‘that’ tells the reader exactly which book James liked: the one that had pictures. This information is necessary to the sentence: the emphasis is on the fact that the book had pictures. In the second sentence, the information after ‘which’ tells the reader additional and non-essential information about the book. The emphasis in this sentence is that James liked the book. The phrase after ‘which’ adds that the book he liked happened to be one with pictures. Note that ‘which’ is typically preceded by a comma, whereas there is no comma before ‘that’. ## **Your or you’re** ‘Your’ is a possessive pronoun, for example: ‘your car’, ‘your phone’, etc. ‘You’re’ is a contraction of ‘you are’, the apostrophe indicating that the ‘a’ is missing, as in: ‘You’re really tall and your spiky hair makes you seem taller!’ ‘Whose’ can refer to both people and organisations, as in: ‘ABN AMRO, whose profits were announced last week...’. Do not confuse with ‘who’s’, which is a contraction of ‘who is’. ## **Worldwide** We write this word as one word (ignore your spell checker if it tells you otherwise). ## **Which or that** The general rule is: ‘that’ defines and ‘which’ informs. Use a footnote to provide additional or background information on a passage in your text, but only if it is impossible to provide the explanation in the text itself. Refer to the footnote by using an asterisk (*). In the event of multiple footnotes, use numbers in superscript (1). Never use footnotes in online texts or emails. ## **Full stops** Use plenty. We don’t want to exclude anyone, and language plays a key role. All it takes is one poorly chosen word or inadequate explanation to give a large target group the feeling the text isn’t meant for them. To make sure your message also gets across to readers who struggle, it’s important to pay attention to things like jargon, length of sentences, structure and style. Check chapter 3, ‘Wording your message’ for more information. ## **6.2 Use gender-neutral language** Attention to gender-neutral, non-sexist language has been on the rise for decades. This has made us increasingly aware of words that can be experienced as biased, discriminatory or derogatory because they suggest that a certain gender is standard. ## **Gender-neutral job titles** A lot of job titles suggest that the job is specifically done by a man or a woman. Something else you see a lot is the word ‘female’ or ‘woman’ added to job titles when the writer apparently associates a certain job title more with men than with women. Whether they do this consciously or subconsciously, it’s certainly wrong, so use a more general, gender-neutral alternative instead. **Gender-specific** Businesswoman Saleswoman **Gender-neutral** Businessperson Salesperson / Sales representative ## **Gender-neutral pronouns** The idea that a person is a man or a woman is deeply engrained in our language. This is clear from our personal pronouns: ‘he’, ‘she’, ‘his’ and ‘her’. You should avoid these words. There are various ways to do this. Let’s take the following sentence as an example: ‘The bank employee should make sure his/her name badge is always visible.’ - **Use a definite article rather than a personal pronoun** - ‘The bank employee should make sure the name badge is always visible.’ - **Address the reader directly** ‘As a bank employee, you should make sure your name badge is always visible.’ - **Use the plural rather than the singular** ‘Bank employees should make sure their name badge is always visible.’ - **Use they/them/their as a singular pronoun. For instance, words that express something individualistic, competitive, ambitious or assertive are generally more appropriate for men. Research shows that women actually respond better to language that creates a sense of community, social and emotional words, dedication, support, compassion and understanding. Women have a clear aversion to masculine texts, while men don’t mind feminine or neutral language. In light of this, opt for more feminine wording where possible. That way, you’ll reach the biggest audience. ## **Use people-first language** You sometimes need to mention a specific group, if you want to say something about people who are deaf or blind, for instance.
